coredata.dc:description: This work describes a novel fluorescent chemoprobe that uses carbon dots and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) to monitor mercury ions in aqueous samples attributed to the principle of inner filter effect. The fluorescent response signal of the carbon dots is diminished by AgNPs, attributed to inner filter effect, and is restored with the addition of Hg2+. The fluorescent chemoprobe was specific over the range from 0.01 to 2.5 μM and a high sensitivity of 3.6 nM. The chemoprobe was validated using real local aqueous samples, and the spike recoveries of 97.4%–103% were excellent and satisfied. The data indicated that the developed fluorescent chemoprobe was sensitive, selective, stable and reliable. This fluorescent chemoprobe provides a sensitive tool with broad prospects for mercury detection in aqueous samples and the work will offer ideas for designing and constructing novel fluorescent probes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work describes a novel fluorescent chemoprobe that uses carbon dots and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) to monitor mercury ions in aqueous samples attributed to the principle of inner filter effect. The fluorescent response signal of the carbon dots is diminished by AgNPs, attributed to inner filter effect, and is restored with the addition of Hg2+. The fluorescent chemoprobe was specific over the range from 0.01 to 2.5 μM and a high sensitivity of 3.6 nM. The chemoprobe was validated using real local aqueous samples, and the spike recoveries of 97.4%–103% were excellent and satisfied. The data indicated that the developed fluorescent chemoprobe was sensitive, selective, stable and reliable. This fluorescent chemoprobe provides a sensitive tool with broad prospects for mercury detection in aqueous samples and the work will offer ideas for designing and constructing novel fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury, a toxic heavy metal and ubiquitous pollutant, has become a worldwide general research hotspot and has been listed as priority pollutant internationally because of its extremely detrimental impact on both various environment and human health. In the environment, mercury comes from a wide range of sources such as discharge of plants, exhaust from coal fired power plants and coal burning industries, smelting of ores, cement production, artisanal gold mining, waste incineration and construction waste. Mercury is commonly present in elemental form, as inorganic mercuric (HgII) compounds or toxic methyl mercury compounds. Inorganic mercury may be transferred into organomercury compounds in environment depending on microorganisms and other factors (Kobal et al., 2017; Gredilla et al., 2019; Saikia et al., 2018; Gasparotto et al., 2018; Sánchez-Peña et al., 2018; Ferrari et al., 2019; Rojas et al., 2019; Oliveira et al., 2019). Generally, mercury at a very low level causes severe impairment of the brain, immune, and central nervous systems as it does not biodegrade well and it easily gradually accumulates in the foodweb (Nolan and Lippard, 2003; Lu et al., 2016; Kundu et al., 2019; Shorie et al., 2019). Until now, various methods have been established to measure the concentration of Hg2+, which include graphite furnace and electro-thermal atomic absorption spectrometry (Zangmo and Siripinyanond, 2019; Thongsaw et al., 2019), electrochemical sensing (Fu et al., 2020), surface-enhanced Raman scattering method (Chen et al., 2018), and isotope dilution inductively coupled plasma mass-spectrometry (Long et al., 2020). Although some of the aforementioned methods offer excellent sensitivity and multielement analysis, these techniques have some limitations in accurate detection of Hg2+ detection, including complex sample preparation, costly instrument facilities, time-consuming procedures, and the requirement of trained personnel (Lin et al., 2010). Thus, the excellent determination of Hg2+ at trace levels in environmental samples via simple and efficient approaches is a critical issue.

Carbon dots (CDs), a newly emerging carbon material with excellent fluorescence, have been a universal hot topic ascribed to their prominent fluorescent characteristics, cheap preparation methods, high photo-stability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility (Sun et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2019; Devi et al., 2019). Due to these distinct benefits, advances have been gained in many applications, such as sensing, anti-counterfeiting, biomedical, drug delivery, ink, optoelectronic devices, and photocatalysis (Bonet-San-Emeterio et al., 2020; Cui et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2019; Pajewska-Szmyt et al., 2020; Sharma et al., 2019; Singh et al., 2017; Sk and Chattopadhyay, 2014; Behera et al., 2019; Martindale et al., 2015). CDs from different carbon sources can act as sensitive chemoprobes for measuring metal ions according to their strong binding and chelating affinity to metal ions due to their abundant surface functional groups, including amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups (Shi et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2016a, 2017a; Song et al., 2017; Pu et al., 2020). Based on the effective interactions, the fluorescent response signal of CDs decreased little by little with raising metal ion concentration. The change of fluorescent response signal was linearly related to the dosage of metal ions over a certain scope, which was used to quantitatively determine the target metal ion content.

The inner filter effect (IFE) can decrease the emitted light via the absorption of emitted radiation due to the presence of absorbers in the detection system, where absorption intensity variations of the absorber can convert into remarkable variations of the fluorophore’s fluorescent emission. In recent studies, the inner filter effect has been explored to construct some sensitive fluorescent methods for determination of analytes (Wang et al., 2020; Al-Hashimi et al., 2020; Muraille et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2020). Compared with other fluorescence quenching methods, the IFE does not require a covalent linkage, which provides considerable flexibility, facility, and low limits of detection (Li et al., 2016; Chang and Ho, 2015). Recently, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are one kind of star metal nano-particles and have also absorbed much attention due to their outstanding merits such as size- and morphology-dependent tuable optical properties and strong surface plasmon resonance. It has achieved good applications in trace determination of metal ions, organic compounds based on spectrophotometry, colorimetry and naked eye detection (Wu et al., 2014; Apyari et al., 2019; Zafer et al., 2020; Jouyban and Rahimpour, 2020) which indicated that AgNPs are good nanomaterials with great potential and prospect.

In this study, a sensitive chemoprobe was designed for determining mercury ions using silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and CDs based on the IFE. The principle of the fluorescent chemoprobe is illuminated in Fig. 1 . The fluorescence response signal of the obtained CDs was remarkably decreased by AgNPs due to the IFE. AgNPs aggregated with the introduction of mercury ions and the absorbance of the AgNPs decreased, which restored the quenched fluorescence of the CDs. This principle was utilized to design a highly sensitive chemoprobe for measuring the mercury ion concentration. Highly dispersed and uniform AgNPs played a remarkable role in the efficiencies of present chemoprobe sensing system.

Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was supplied by Guangfu Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). The main chemicals used are as the same as reported our previous study (Liu et al., 2017a). All other reagents were of analytical grade.

A UV-2600 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China) was utilized to measure ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra. The fluorescent lifetimes were examined on an FLS980 fluorescent spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, Edinburgh, UK). The other instruments used for characterization were as the same as reported in our previous study (Liu et al., 2017a).

CDs were fabricated using citric acid and polyacrylamide based on a facile hydro-thermal process. The polyacrylamide was prepared according to a previous report (Liu et al., 2017a). For the preparation of CDs, 966 mg citric acid and 521 mg polyacrylamide were dispersed in 20 mL pure water and stirred vigorously. The solution was shifted into a PTFE reactor and held at 200 °C for 3 h. A yellowish brown mixture was achieved, and further the CDs were attained after further dialyzing and drying. Finally, the CDs were used to prepare a 0.05 mg mL−1 standard solution with ultrapure water.

AgNPs were produced using NaBH4 acting as a reductant in the presence of PVP as a stabilizer. All of the glassware was comprehensively pre-cleaned with aquaregia and washed with purified water. About 1 mL AgNO3 (10 mM) and 1 mL PVP (4 mg mL−1) were diffused into a certain amount of water with constant stirring in an ice-water bath. After 20 min, 1 mL cold and freshly prepared NaBH4 (3.8 mg mL−1) was dropped to the solution under stirring and the mixture became yellow. After continuously stirring for 5 min in the ice-water bath and another 15 min at ambient temperature, brownish yellow AgNPs were formed. AgNPs solution is kept in the refrigerator at 4 °C. Then, the AgNPs were tested with a transmission-electron microscopy (TEM). The Mie-theory-based formula was used to determine the molar extinction coefficient (ε) of the AgNPs (Navarro and Werts, 2013).

The determination of the mercury ion concentration was achieved on an F-7000 fluorescent spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan), and all of the fluorescent spectra were recorded with the emission and excitation slit widths at 5 nm at room temperature. The excitation wavelength was kept at 320 nm. About 30 μL CD solution and 100 μL AgNP solution were transferred into a centrifugal tube and further increased to 1 mL using 10 mM phosphate buffer with a pH of 7.0, which was used as a control. Next, different amounts of Hg2+ were dropped to a 30 μL CDs and 100 μL AgNPs solution, and the mixture was increased to 1 mL with 10 mM phosphate buffer with a pH of 7.0 and mixed completely. The fluorescent intensities were measured before and after dropping of Hg2+. Twenty cations were investigated to validate the selectivity for Hg2+.

The reliability of the probe was very important in this study. In this experiment, four different aqueous samples were collected and utilized to verify the fluorescent chemoprobe. The aqueous samples were sampled from Binhe park, Changping park, Chaobai river and Mina Tombs Reservoir (Beijing, China). These aqueous samples were first pretreated with 0.22-μm microporous membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CDs and AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: After the carbon sources were hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 3 h, the CDs were obtained and the TEM was as shown in Fig. 2 A. It was obvious that the obtained CDs possessed good dispersion and had sphere-like structures with a mean diameter of approximate 4.1 nm. The TEM image of AgNPs (Fig. 2B) showed that they are orbicular and had good dispersibility with a mean diameter of 7.1 nm. The FT-IR spectrum was further investigated to confirm the surface functional groups of the as-prepared CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 2C, three featured bands centered at 3418, 3275 and 1439 cm−1 are ascribed to the extensional vibrations of O–H/N–H and N–H and the flexural vibration of O–H, respectively. Moreover, the band centered at 2945 cm−1 is related to the extensional vibrations of C–H. The sharp band at 1716 cm−1 indicated the existence of CO. The bands near 1551, 1223 and 1169 cm−1 accounted for the flexural vibration of N–H/CC and the extensional vibrations of C–N and C–O, respectively (Liu et al., 2017a). The FT-IR data demonstrated that there were many hydrophilic groups on the surface of these prepared CDs. The presence of hydrophilic groups not only improved the water solubility but also enhanced the stability of the obtained CDs. What’s more, the strong fluorescence was derived from many flaws acted as excited energy traps on the surface of the CDs that was owing to the rich hydrophilic functional groups (Wang et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Main features of the prepared CDs and AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A shows that the prepared CDs displayed obvious absorbance bands at 230 and 325 nm. The absorbance band centered at 230 nm was ascribed to the CC bond π-π∗ transition, and the absorbance band centered at 325 nm is assigned to the n-π∗ transition dependent on the generation of emission traps and excitation surface states, that were be favor for the fluorescence enhancement (Liu et al., 2017a). Moreover, the absorption spectrum showed an absorbance band at 396 nm, that is the particular peak of AgNPs (Khan et al., 2012). Fig. 3B depicts a 3D plot of excitation and emission wavelengths. The different colours denoted different fluorescence intensities of the prepared CDs at different excitation wavelengths. It is obvious that the maxima fluorescent emission intensity of the CDs is obtained at 415 nm when setting the excitation wavelength at 320 nm. Meanwhile, the excitation-independent multicolor emission feature is clearly displayed in Fig. 3C. Fig. 3C indicates that the corresponding fluorescent emission peaks of most CDs are maintained at 415 nm without red-shifting over excitation wavelengths from 280 to 340 nm. This was ascribed to the similar sizes of the CDs, which is consistent with TEM consequences (Wang et al., 2014). The relative quantum yield of the CDs was examined to be 12.6%, which are similar to some of the CDs in the reports (Shu et al., 2017; Campos et al., 2016; Li et al., 2017).

Fig. 3D exhibits the strongly pH-sensitive characteristics of the CDs. The fluorescent emission intensity of the CDs risen to the largest value at pH 9.0, with solution pH changing from 1 to 9, and then decreased obviously as the pH changed from 9 to 14. This may be attributed to the plentiful functional groups with negative charge (Rong et al., 2017). More interestingly, the fluorescent intensity signal of the CDs demonstrated an excellent linearity with pH value over ranges of 1–9 (inset in Fig. 3D (a)) and 9–14 (inset in Fig. 3D (b)), which indicates that the CDs could be applied for pH sensing in the range of 1–14. As seen from Fig. 3E, the CDs exhibited high stability over a wide ionic strength scope, with no visible changes of the fluorescence intensity found even at a high NaCl concentration of 4.5 M. These results reveal that the fluorescence intensity of CDs is not affected by ionic strength in a large concentration range and the CDs have good application prospects in physiological fields (Shi et al., 2015). In addition, no deposited or suspended particles are found in the CDs solution even after three months and the fluorescence emission intensity shows no significant changes, which illuminates that the obtained CDs possess much strong homogeneity and fluorescence stability (Fig. 3F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent emission features of the CDs and the sensing principle
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 A exhibits the different fluorescence characteristics of the CDs under various conditions. The fluorescent emission intensity of the prepared CDs was noticeably declined in the existence of AgNPs, and that was not affected by only the presence of Hg2+ which suggesting that there was no direct interaction between the CDs and Hg2+. However, the quenched fluorescence from AgNPs was further recovered with the continuous addition of Hg2+. This phenomenon indicated the system containing CDs and AgNPs could be used as a chemoprobe to detect Hg2+. Meanwhile, the fluorescent intensity declined by AgNPs was efficiently recovered by adding Hg2+ with no identifiable change in the emission, which indicates that the restored fluorescence intensity resulted from the CDs instead of freshly generated emissive groups. To study the detection mechanism, the measurements of UV–vis spectra, fluorescence lifetimes and TEM were carried out. As exhibited from Fig. 4B, the UV–vis spectrum of AgNPs is broadly covered by the emission spectrum of the prepared CDs which indicates that the decline of fluorescence could be attributed to the IFE or FRET. So as to determine the primary cause of fluorescence quenched mechanism, the fluorescent lifetimes of the prepared CDs were measured (Fig. 4C). The mean lifetimes of three mixtures including prepared CDs, prepared CDs + AgNPs, and prepared CDs + AgNP + Hg2+ were 8.76, 8.87 and 8.82 ns, accordingly. The mean lifetime of the CDs had almost no change due to the presence of AgNPs and Hg2+. In contrast to FRET, two substances in the IFE system would not chemically interreact and, therefore, the quenching reagent would not influence the fluorescent lifetime of the fluorescence reagent (Lin et al., 2015). Hence, the results show that the quenching was not due to an FRET mechanism, and that IFE would be a reliable explanation to the achieved fluorescence decline.

As known, the particular ultraviolet–visible peak of AgNPs is rather closely linked to their inter-particle distance. When the inter-particle distance of AgNPs changes, the absorption peak will also shift remarkably (Vilela et al., 2012). From Fig. 4D, it is obvious that the absorbance of AgNPs decreased in the presence of Hg2+. AgNPs can reduce Hg2+ to Hg, and then the AgNPs and Hg form a AgHg amalgam, which results in the separation of AgNPs and PVP, leading to further aggregation of the AgNPs (Ramesh and Radhakrishnan, 2011; Liu et al., 2017b). This result was further affirmed by TEM of AgNPs with presence of Hg2+ (Fig. 4E), which showed an obvious aggregation of AgNPs that were rather dispersed without Hg2+ (Fig. 2B). The TEM results matched with the altered UV–vis spectra.

As mentioned above, the IFE between the CDs and AgNPs was weakened by the decreased absorbance of the AgNPs due to increased amounts of Hg2+, and the quenched fluorescence emission intensity of the chemoprobe was then restored. Therefore, a fluorescent chemoprobe from CDs and AgNPs will be utilized for the measurement of Hg2+ by monitoring fluorescence.

For achieving better sensitivity for Hg2+ detection, some important factors, including the dosage of CDs, the dosage of AgNPs, phosphate buffer pH, and the reaction time, were optimized. Throughout the investigating procedure, Hg2+ was kept at 2.0 μM. The ΔF (ΔF = F2–F1, in which F1 and F2 are fluorescent emission intensity of this chemoprobe without or with Hg2+, respectively) value is used as the Hg2+ signal.

Usually, keeping a constant amount of Hg2+, a less dosage of CDs resulted in a lower limit of detection (LOD), while a larger dosage gave a wider linear scope (Liu et al., 2017a). It is issue to optimize the dosage of CDs for gaining better analytical results of Hg2+. Fig. 5 A shows the effect of the dosage of CDs on the determination of Hg2+. The results indicate that ΔF fortified markedly as the dosage of CDs increased and reached a peak with no apparent change at 1.5 μg mL−1. Hence, 1.5 μg mL−1 was used as the optimal CDs dosage for detecting Hg2+ in the following experimental procedures.

It is very similar to optimize the influence of CDs, a much larger dosage of AgNPs could reduce the fluorescent emission intensity of the CDs, but the signal for Hg2+ at low concentrations was weaker. A small dosage of AgNPs could enhance the Hg2+ signal, but too strong of a background fluorescence resulted in a much narrower linear range. As expected, the dosage of AgNPs has a great effect on the ΔF value (Fig. 5B). The optimal performance was achieved at 1.2 nM of AgNPs. So, the dosage of AgNPs was chosen to be 1.2 nM.

The effect of pH is displayed in Fig. 5C. The ΔF value rose rapidly to a peak at the pH vaue of 7.0, and then declined as the pH value of sample solution was over pH 7. In a strong acidic environment, there are plenty hydrogen ions which may interreact with carboxylate groups from the CDs, which will result in a reduced interreaction over the CDs and AgNPs. In a strong alkaline environment, Hg2+ is easily hydrolyzed and may react with rich OH− ions instead of AgNPs in the solution (Fu et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2017), which can lessen the interreaction probability between Hg2+ and AgNPs, reducing the fluorescence recovery and causing a decreased ΔF. Therefore, pH value of the sample was adjusted to pH 7.

The settling time can significantly impact the performance of the chemoprobe, which needs to be considered for the application of the chemoprobe. The effect of the settling time on ΔF was also optimized. Fig. 5D illustrates that the quenched fluorescent emission of the chemoprobe is gradually restored by Hg2+ as time progresses and remains almost unchanged in 6 min, which indicated the detection reaction can reach equilibrium within 6 min. In order to obtain better fluorescence signal and a complete interaction, 8 min was applied as the optimal settling time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feature analytical parameters of this chemoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: With the optioned experimental parameters, the feature analytical parameters of the chemoprobe was investigated in the presence of mercury ions. As exhibited in Fig. 6 A, the quenched fluorescent emission of the chemoprobe recovered as the dosage of mercury ions increased over the range from 0.01 to 3.5 μM. It was obvious that the ΔF value of the chemoprobe demonstrated excellent linearity within the mercury ion concentration of 0.01–2.5 μM (Fig. 6B). The LOD of the chemoprobe was determined according to 3σ/s principle, in which σ is standard deviation obtained from 25 control determinations and s represents the slope of the linearity regression curve, which was 3.6 nM. Li and coworkers prosed a novel chemoprobe based on N,S-CDs from citric acid, urea and l-cysteine by using microwave-assisted method. The LOD of the constructed chemprobe was 2 μM (Li et al., 2015). Li et al. prepared −OOC-CDs served as the chemoprobe for sensing Hg2+ with a LOD of 5.7 μM (Li et al., 2017). The above-mentioned and other developed chemoprobes based on CDs were compared, and the detailed results are exhibited in Table 1 . Compared to some previous reports, the preparation of CDs was relatively simple and rapid. What’s more, the sensitivity of the developed chemoprobe was equal or much more superior than that of some previous reports, which indicated that it was a good tool for the determination of Hg2+ concentration.

The selectivity of the proposed fluorescent chemoprobe is a crucial issue for sensing Hg2+ in a complex sample. The results of the Hg2+ sensing should not be affected by coexisting ions. First, keeping the concentration of Hg2+ at 0.1 μM, the concentrations of 17 popular cations were individually adjusted to 3.0 μM, the concentration of Ag+ was adjusted to 2.0 μM, and the concentrations of sodium and potassium ions were individually adjusted to 1.0 mM. Then, the fluorescent changes of the fluorescent chemoprobe were studied and are exhibited in Fig. 7 . From Fig. 7 (blue bars), the fluorescence emission intensity was kept almost constant for cations other than Hg2+, which proved that the selectivity of the developed fluorescent chemoprobe was very high, giving it a great application prospect in the sensitive detection of mercury ions. Alternatively, the same amounts of mercury ions and the cations mentioned above were added into the chemoprobe solution. It was apparent that the added cations resulted in a negligible impact on the recovering effect of Hg2+ (Fig. 7, red bars), which strongly suggests that this proposed fluorescent chemoprobe is unprecedentedly selective to Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accuracy and stability for sensing Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: As far as a fluorescent chemoprobe is concerned, it is very important for it to be stable and reliable, which determines whether it can be widely used in the future. For the sake of these proposes, the precisions and stability of the chemoprobe were investigated. In order to obtain the intra-day precision (IntraP), the fluorescent intensity changes of the chemoprobe with Hg2+ spiked at 0.025, 0.05 and 0.1 μM were acquired six times a day, whereas the fluorescent intensity changes of the chemoprobe with Hg2+ at the same levels were acquired each time for six continuous days to calculate the inter-day precision (InterP). The experimental results exhibited that the IntraP values are 4.2, 3.9, and 2.0% (n = 6) and InterP values are 3.9, 2.6, and 1.8% (n = 6) with Hg2+ at 0.025, 0.050 and 0.10 μM, respectively. The fluorescence responses of the developed chemoprobe with Hg2+ at 0.10 μM were recorded at fixed intervals for 24 h ΔF values are shown in Fig. S1, which exhibited no remarkable difference. These results indicate that the fluorescent chemoprobe was highly stable in the analytical process and could be used for a long time, which affirmed its repeatability for sensing Hg2+.

Mercury (Hg) is a global pollutant and one of the most toxic metals which threatens human and ecosystem health, and also considered as a priority hazardous substance. Mercury emission occurred from various ways and sectors, and which are different in different regions. Most of the emissions appeared in Asia (49%, of which 39% in East and South-east Asia), followed by South America (18%) and Sub-Saharan Africa (16%)(UN Environment, 2019). In the 20th century, industrial effluents containing mercury were released into lakes and caused over a thousand of cases of mercury poisoning in Minamata, Japan. The known Minamata disease in the world was due to the methylmercury poisoning (Takaoka et al., 2018). Waste water containing mercury from a chemical plant was released into water supply, and finally reached the food chain of the local Grassy Narrows Indian population for generations in Ontario, Canada (Budnik and Casteleyn, 2019). Total global Hg emissions to the atmosphere in the range of 6500–8200 Mg yr−1, of which 4600–5300 Mg yr−1 come from natural processes and sources (primary geogenic plus secondary emissions) (Driscoll et al., 2013) and the dominant share of mercury emissions is from man-made sources. According to United Nations Environment Program (AMAP/UN Environment Expert Group, 2019) the anthropogenic sources are responsible for more than 2220 tons of mercury in air in 2015 (Baimenov et al., 2020). Zhang et al. found that Hg had great potential ecological risk to the southern alluvial plain of the lower Yellow River in China (Zhang et al., 2018). It was found that mercury pollution was ubiquitous and very serious in some regions in the world. Hence it is of great value to construct sensitive sensors for detecting Hg2+ at trace levels in environmental samples. Proposed sensor is developed in this situation. In order to assess the applicability of the developed chemoprobe, four water samples were taken from local regions to analyze. The analytical experimental results of the control and spiked aqueous samples are shown in Table 2 . From Table 2, it was obvious that the spiked recoveries of Hg2+ were very good, with all over 97.4%. The RSDs were below 4.4%, which revealed that the proposed chemoprobe is a reliable analytical tool for Hg2+ detection in the environment and other fields.

In the present work, strongly fluorescent CDs were synthesized using citrate acid as well as polyacrylamide by a simple, convenient and solvothermal treatment means. The IFE between CDs and AgNPs was effectively utilized for developing a highly reliable chemoprobe for determination of mercury ion. The achieved fluorescent chemoprobe had high sensitivity and its LOD was as low as 3.6 nM. Validation experiments resulted in excellent spike recoveries of 97.4%–103%. These experimental data show that this developed chemoprobe is a credible and sensitive alternative for determination of mercury ion, and could become a very promising trace analysis platform for broad use in various fields. It’s expected that the proposed chemoprobe can be utilized to establish an efficient ratiometric fluorescence sensor with other CDs or fluorescent materials, or to construct fluorescent paper strips to realize on-site real sample determination in the future.

Qingxiang Zhou: Conceptualization, Supervision, Visualization, Investigation. Yongli Liu: Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing-Original draft preparation. Yalin Wu: Methodology, Software. Zhi Li: Writing-review and editing. Yanhui Li: Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation. Menghua Liu: Resource, Investigation. Tongxu Qu: Software. Chunmao Chen: Writing-review and editing.
